description: The mouse: the smallest housemate, quick along the wall, quiet under the floor, gone before the eye finishes turning. It lives at the edge of every granary and hearth, taking little, seen rarely, the fable-teller's favorite small hero. 'Misuki' names the creature without verdict: not vermin, a neighbor of the smallest size.
sound_symbolism: 'mi' small, 'su' soft, 'ki' quick: the whole animal in three light syllables, narrow vowels for a narrow body, ending on the little stop of a creature vanishing into its hole.
grammatical_notes: Noun. 'misuki phou tomae maeli roke.' (The mouse moves quietly under the floor.) A counted mouse may take optional 'lipha', the classifier of non-human living things. Stands in the household ring beside 'misheko' (cat) and 'lohau' (dog), sharing the cat's soft 'mi' and none of its size.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A value-neutral name for a creature usually cursed on sight: the smallest test of speaking without contempt.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Granary, hearth, and field all know the mouse; the fables that teach smallness its dignity are told on its back.
